Now, the same experience can be extended to customer-facing teams by integrating SAP Service Cloud Version 2 (V2) into SAP Build Work Zone, enabling service agents and managers to work from a single, personalized workspace.

In this blog, we will explore the business value, architecture, implementation approach, and a demo scenario showcasing how SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Service Cloud V2 can work together seamlessly.

A Short Story

Meet Prasad, a Customer Service Manager at a global manufacturing company running SAP's full suite.

Every morning, Prasad logs into SAP Build Work Zone. On her home page, she sees her personalized tiles: open production orders from S/4HANA, pending approvals from SuccessFactors, and her team's KPI dashboard. Everything is in one place.

But then her phone rings. A key customer is escalating a service ticket — a machine breakdown at their site. Prasad switches to a completely different browser tab, logs into SAP Service Cloud V2 separately, searches for the customer, finds the ticket, checks the service history, and tries to correlate it with the spare parts availability from S/4HANA — which means another tab, another login.

Three systems. Three tabs. One frustrated manager. One unhappy customer.

This is the problem SAP Build Work Zone + SAP Service Cloud V2 integration solves.

After the integration, Prasad's Work Zone home page now has a dedicated Service Operations section. The Service Cloud V2 app is embedded as a tile and a full sidebar integration. She can:

  • See real-time ticket queue directly from the Work Zone homepage
  • Open and update service tickets without leaving Work Zone
  • WorkZone can Receive intelligent notifications when ticket SLA thresholds are breached

Her morning is now seamless. Her customer gets faster resolution. Her team is more productive.

That is the power of a single entry point.

Key Benefits

True Single Entry Point for All Personas

Persona                                                             Before                                                                          After

Service Agent

2 systems (Work Zone + Service Cloud)

1 — Work Zone

Service Manager

3 systems (Work Zone + Service Cloud + S/4HANA)

1 — Work Zone

IT Admin

Manage 3 separate portals

Manage 1 unified portal

Executive

Multiple dashboards

1 personalized command center

Key Benefits

  • Unified User Experience:  Access SAP Service Cloud V2, SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and custom applications through one portal.
  • Single Sign-On :Reduce authentication complexity and improve user productivity.
  • Role-Based Access: Deliver personalized content, applications, and workflows based on user roles.
  • Improved Productivity: Minimize context switching between applications and browser tabs.
  • Extensibility: Integrate custom SAPUI5 and SAP BTP applications alongside standard SAP solutions.

Why bring Service Cloud V2 into Work Zone

 

How to surface SAP Service Cloud V2 in Work Zone

  • Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard or advanced edition

In your BTP subaccount, go to Service Marketplace and subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone. Advanced Edition is required if you need Digital Workplace Service features (content federation, external solutions). Standard is sufficient for most SAP Cloud app integrations.

 
Navigation path: BTP Cockpit → Subaccount → Service Marketplace → SAP Build Work Zone → Create subscription → choose edition → Assign role collections Launchpad_Admin and Launchpad_User to relevant users.

  • Create a destination for SAP Service Cloud V2

Work Zone discovers external SaaS apps through BTP Destinations. SAP Service Cloud V2 exposes OData and REST APIs under its tenant URL. Create a destination with OAuth2 client credentials so Work Zone can fetch app descriptors and launch URLs securely.

Destination config (key fields)

Name: SCV2
Type: HTTP · URL: https://<your-tenant>.crm.cloud.sap
Auth: OAuth2ClientCredentials
Token Service URL: IAS token endpoint
Additional Properties: HTML5.DynamicDestination = true, sap-platform = cf

  • Add Service Cloud V2 as a content provider in Work Zone

Now tell Work Zone where to find those deployed apps. Open the Work Zone Site Manager and register Service Cloud V2 as a content provider. Point it at the HTML5 App Repository app and the SCV2 destination. Work Zone will fetch the CDM and expose the tiles to administrators.

Navigation path

Work Zone Site Manager → Channel Manager → Add → SAP BTP → Content Provider Name: SAP Service Cloud V2 → Runtime Destination: SCV2 → Design-Time Destination: SCV2 → Fetch content → Save.

  • Assign Service Cloud tiles to roles and groups

After fetching content, the Service Cloud V2 tiles appear in the Content Manager under "Available Content". Assign them to a role (e.g., ServiceAgent) and add that role to a launchpad group. 

Navigation path : Work Zone Content Manager → Add to Role → Create/select role ServiceAgent → Assign group Service Cloud Apps → Publish site.
 

See it in action — one launchpad, everything connected

To demonstrate the integration capabilities of SAP Build Work Zone, i created a simple demo showcasing how different applications can be brought together through a unified user experience.

In this video , users can observe the following:

  • Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Integration
    SAP Build Work Zone provides direct access to Sales and Service Cloud V2, allowing users to launch these applications seamlessly from a single location.
  • Access to Business Objects
    Users can navigate to important business objects such as Accounts and Cases directly from Work Zone, reducing the need to switch between multiple systems.
  • Custom Application Integration
    Custom applications, including SAPUI5 applications deployed on SAP BTP, can be embedded within Work Zone and accessed alongside standard SAP applications.
  • Cross-Application Navigation
    The same custom application embedded in Work Zone can also be accessed from Service Cloud V2, enabling a connected and consistent user journey across platforms.

Why This Matters

By bringing together SAP applications and custom solutions, SAP Build Work Zone becomes a true single entry point for users. Whether users need to access Service Cloud V2, Sales Cloud, business data, or custom applications, everything is available through a unified digital workplace.

This approach simplifies navigation, improves productivity, and delivers a more seamless user experience by reducing the need to manage multiple entry points and disconnected systems.

Conclusion

Organizations often struggle with users navigating across multiple applications and portals. With SAP Build Work Zone, businesses can create a centralized experience that connects SAP solutions and custom applications under one roof. The result is a more intuitive, efficient, and user-friendly workspace that helps employees focus on their work rather than searching for the right application.

entry.title: Operationalizing Autonomous CX with the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced Autonomous CX as a core pillar of the Autonomous Enterprise. The principle that every customer promise must be backed by operational reality. The SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience, part of the SAP Services and Support portfolio, is the expert-led engagement model that translates that vision into execution, helping organizations adopt, activate, and scale the SAP CX and AI innovations announced at Sapphire.

The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience is a proactive, expert led engagement model built to derisk transformation, accelerate time to value, and sustain measurable outcomes across CX initiatives. It combines guided adoption, prescriptive functional and technical assistance, AI powered best practices, and continuous value realization aligned to the realities of modern CX: AI at the core, unified data, omnichannel at scale, retention over acquisition, service led growth, and persistent skills gaps in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.

 

At its heart, the plan brings together the right expertise at the right time; program governance, solution experts, value advisors, and adoption specialists. This helps your teams execute faster and smarter with SAP Customer Experience.

 

What Sets the Advanced Success Plan Apart

 

  • Outcome-based: Business outcomes and key value indicators are co-defined with your teams, with milestones and workstreams aligned to deliver measurable Autonomous CX results.
  • Proactive by design: AI Assistants, adoption checks, and innovation accelerators are embedded throughout, reducing risk and compressing time to value as agentic capabilities evolve.
  • Continuous enablement: Role-based enablement and coaching are tied directly to your Autonomous CX roadmap closing skills gaps at pace as new AI and platform capabilities become available.
  • Cross-solution orchestration: Unified processes and shared business context across marketing, commerce, sales and service break silos and enable enterprise-scale execution.

 

This is the first of a summer series where we will go deep on one of the topics below. Today we will start with an introduction on how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience helps you operationalize seven macro trends shaping modern CX.

 

1) AI‑Powered Customer Experiences

AI now underpins everything from next best engagement to intelligent service resolution. The Advanced Success Plan embeds AI adoption patterns directly into your delivery approach; identifying high value use cases, calibrating data prerequisites, and guiding model governance.

The result; Prioritization of high‑impact starting points, a plan to scale with guardrails, accelerating time from pilot to production and grounding every decision in SAP’s CX AI capabilities and product roadmap.

 

 

2) Hyper‑Personalization at Scale

Personalization demands more than algorithms; it requires clean, consent‑aware data, robust decisioning, and experimentation discipline. The Advanced Success Plan delivers:

- Data readiness assessments and integration patterns to enrich customer profiles and segments

- Governance and testing playbooks to validate personalization hypotheses at scale

- Prescriptive journeys to operationalize next best action across every customer channel

The result: hyper personalization moves from proof of concept to standard operating model.

 

3) Unified Customer Data & Breaking Down Silos

Siloed data undermines CX. We help you establish a unified data foundation and harmonized identities, aligning business, data, and integration teams. With technical guidance and adoption accelerators, you can move faster toward a single view of the customer to fuel analytics, personalization, and service excellence:

The result: Unified profile use cases, data quality baselines, and source‑of‑truth decisions to reduce duplication and latency

 

4) Omnichannel Commerce & B2B Digital Transformation

Modern buyers expect seamless journeys across web, mobile, marketplace, and partner portals, especially in B2B. The plan accelerates omnichannel capability build‑out by uniting commerce, order sourcing, pricing, and fulfillment patterns, supported by outcome‑based governance.

The result: Channel consistency, catalog and contract complexity, and the alignment of service and sales motions are all addressed, driving measurable improvement in conversion rates and repeat purchase.

 

5) Customer Retention Over Acquisition

Acquisition costs are rising; retention is the new growth engine. The Advanced Success Plan helps you operationalize retention strategies, churn prediction, intelligent engagement, loyalty, and proactive service across your CX stack.

The result: We align metrics such as retention rate, customer lifetime value, and service‑to‑revenue contribution, and ensure your data foundation supports them.

 

6) Service as a Revenue Driver

Service is no longer a cost center; it’s a growth channel. We guide you to productize services, monetize value‑added offerings, and embed outcome‑based contracts. The plan includes:

- Playbooks for cross‑sell/upsell from service interactions

- Knowledge and field service patterns to improve first‑time fix and attach rates

- KPI frameworks for service‑led growth

The result: With prescriptive governance and AI‑driven intelligence, service organizations move from reactive cost management to consistent, measurable contribution to top‑line revenue and customer retention.

 

7) Navigating Digital Transformation Complexity & Skills Gaps

Large transformation programs falter on orchestration and capability enablement. The Advanced Success Plan addresses both by:

- Establishing a cadence of value sprints and decision forums

- Providing role‑based enablement covering functional and technical assistance, data, product ownership, end-user adoption, and change management

- AI-guided best practices embedded throughout delivery to eliminate rework and accelerate quality outcomes across Industry AI scenarios

 

Organizations execute with confidence — even amid shifting requirements, resource constraints, and rapidly evolving agentic AI capabilities.

 

Measurable Outcomes

- Accelerated time to first value through prioritized, AI-ready use cases aligned to Autonomous CX capabilities

- Higher adoption and sustained performance via continuous enablement

- Reduced program risk through proactive governance, telemetry, and structured decision forums

- Measurable gains in conversion rates, customer retention, and service-led revenue contribution across the full CX stack

 

Getting Started

1) Define your Autonomous CX priorities: Identify two to three priority outcomes for the next two quarters facilitated by the Value Management service.

2) Assess readiness: Evaluate data, integration, governance, and enablement gaps to define a 12–18 month Engagement Plan.

3) Engage the Advanced Success Plan: Align workstreams, milestones, and metrics with our expert team. 

4) Industrialize and scale: Convert proven delivery patterns into reusable accelerators, deployable across regions and lines of business.

 

In the weeks ahead, each edition of this series will examine one of the seven trends in depth. Demonstrating how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience translates CX strategy into repeatable execution and measurable business outcomes.

entry.title: How the Advanced Success Plan for SAP CX Operationalizes Hyper-Personalization at Scale
entry.content:
The Gap Between Personalization Strategy and Execution

Most organizations have accepted that personalization is no longer a differentiator — it is table stakes. Customers expect brands to know them, anticipate their needs, and engage them with relevance across every channel and touchpoint. The challenge is not agreeing on the vision. The challenge is closing the gap between that vision and systematic, scalable execution.

For many Commerce Cloud and Engagement Cloud customers, this gap shows up in familiar ways: recommendation engines that surface generic results because behavioural data is not connected, email campaigns timed by calendar rather than by individual habit, loyalty programs that reward transactions rather than relationships, and personalization rules that require significant manual effort to maintain at scale.

The ambition is there. The infrastructure to fulfil it, however, is often partial — clean data is siloed, AI capabilities are underutilised, and the organisational discipline to run ongoing experimentation does not yet exist.

This is precisely the problem the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience is designed to solve.

 

What Hyper-Personalization Actually Requires

True hyper-personalization is not a feature you switch on. It is a capability you build systematically across three interdependent layers: data, decisioning, and delivery.

 

  1. Data is the foundation. Hyper-personalization requires unified, consent-aware, real-time customer profiles — consolidated across commerce transactions, engagement history, browsing behaviour, service interactions, and loyalty activity. Without this foundation, even the most sophisticated AI models are operating on incomplete signals.
  2. Decisioning is where AI translates those signals into action — the next best product to surface, the right offer to present, the optimal moment to reach out. This layer requires not just model accuracy but governance: knowing when to trust the algorithm and when human judgment should override it.
  3. Delivery is where the personalized experience reaches the customer — on the storefront, in the inbox, through a mobile push, across a loyalty interaction. This layer requires orchestration across channels, consistent with the customer's current context.

 

The Advanced Success Plan addresses all three layers simultaneously, providing the expert guidance, governance frameworks, and adoption acceleration needed to move from point capabilities to an integrated operating model.

 

Hyper-Personalization in SAP Commerce Cloud

SAP Commerce Cloud provides the storefront execution layer for personalization at scale. The platform's AI-assisted product recommendations capability enables organizations to show the most relevant products to each visitor at the right point in their shopping journey — from trending products and related items to complimentary products that support cross-sell and upsell motions. This goes beyond manual merchandising rules; it responds dynamically to real-time behavioural signals, improving conversion performance and driving product discovery at a scale no merchandising team could replicate manually.

Yet many Commerce Cloud customers have not yet activated the full depth of these capabilities. The blockers are predictable: data quality gaps that limit recommendation model performance, integration complexity between the commerce layer and upstream profile data, and an absence of the experimentation discipline needed to tune and improve models over time.

The Advanced Success Plan brings targeted guidance to address exactly these barriers. Data readiness assessments establish the quality baselines and integration patterns required to feed reliable signals into Commerce Cloud's personalization engine. Adoption accelerators help teams operationalize experimentation — defining hypotheses, running A/B tests, and translating results into durable configuration changes. The outcome is a storefront that continuously learns and improves, rather than one frozen at the point of initial configuration.

Hyper-Personalization in SAP Engagement Cloud

SAP Engagement Cloud (powered by SAP Emarsys) extends personalization beyond the storefront and into the full lifecycle of the customer relationship. This is where Commerce Cloud's transactional signals combine with engagement history to power cross-channel personalization that is genuinely individual rather than segment-based.

The platform's AI-assisted send time optimization is a direct example of this philosophy in practice. Rather than sending campaigns on a fixed schedule, the capability analyzes each contact's behavioural patterns — independently of time zone, language, or region — and delivers messages at the precise time each individual is most likely to engage. This is not personalization as a concept; it is personalization as an automated, scalable operational process.

Paired with AI-assisted campaign translation and omnichannel orchestration, Engagement Cloud enables marketing teams to move from building campaigns to orchestrating journeys — where the system is continuously learning which signals should trigger which interactions, and adapting those interactions based on what drives response.

The native integration between SAP Commerce Cloud and SAP Emarsys Customer Engagement is a critical accelerator here. By unifying commerce behaviour and engagement data, organizations can drive increases in conversion rate, purchase frequency, and average order value in ways that neither system could achieve independently. The Advanced Success Plan helps customers realize this joint value by aligning integration architecture, data governance, and adoption milestones across both products within a single, coordinated engagement model.

How the Advanced Success Plan Makes It Continuous Improvement

Hyper-personalization projects are often treated as one-time implementations. The Advanced Success Plan is designed to make them repeatable, continuously improving programs. This means:

  • Outcome-based governance: Co-defining the KPIs that matter — conversion rate lift, repeat purchase rate, engagement open rates, average order value — and building work streams aligned to move them measurably.
  • Prescriptive adoption patterns: Structured play-books for activating AI-assisted recommendations, send time optimization, and next-best action logic, with clear milestones and measurable gates.
  • Continuous enablement: Role-based coaching for the teams responsible for data, product ownership, and campaign operations — closing skills gaps that otherwise cause personalization programs to plateau or regress.
  • Proactive telemetry: Regular adoption checks that surface underperforming configurations before they impact business outcomes, and AI-guided best practices that inform ongoing tuning.

 

Making the Business Case Concrete

For Commerce Cloud customers, the value of operationalized hyper-personalization shows in storefront metrics: higher conversion from AI-surfaced recommendations, increased average order value through intelligent cross-sell, and improved product discovery that reduces bounce and exit rates.

For Engagement Cloud customers, the value shows in engagement quality: open rates and click-through rates that reflect individual relevance rather than list-wide broadcast, improved campaign ROI through AI-optimized delivery, and loyalty program engagement that reflects relationship depth rather than transaction volume.

Across both, the compounding effect of unified data and orchestrated decisioning is what transforms hyper-personalization from a POC into a sustained growth mechanism — one that gets measurably better over time.

 

Next in the Series

In our next post, we will examine how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience addresses Unified Customer Data and the challenge of breaking down silos across marketing, commerce, sales, and service. Stay tuned.

entry.title: Customer Retention over acquisition - What the SAP Advanced Success Plan offers utilities customers
entry.content:
The utilities industry is undergoing its most significant transformation in decades. The shift to renewables, smart metering, distributed energy resources, and expanding deregulation are reshaping the relationship between utility companies and their customers. Customer experience is no longer a back-office concern, it is a front-line competitive differentiator.

The SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience is designed precisely for this moment. It offers utilities customers a structured, continuously guided approach to maximizing value from SAP CX investments; from first adoption through to AI-enabled optimization. In this post, we walk through every available service, explain what each one does, and connect it to the realities utilities organizations face today.

A Sector in Motion: Why Customer Experience Has Become Strategic for Utilities

Four converging forces are making customer experience a boardroom priority for utilities and increasing the demand for structured, expert-led adoption support.

Energy transition and service complexity: Renewables, EV charging, solar feed-in tariffs, and demand response programs are adding new service dimensions. Customers expect their energy provider to manage this complexity seamlessly.

Deregulation and customer switching: In liberalized energy markets, customers can, and do, switch providers. The cost of acquisition consistently exceeds the cost of retention. Superior service experience is a measurable retention lever.

Smart metering and data volume: Smart meter rollouts generate billions of interval readings daily. This data can fuel proactive outreach and personalized billing, but only if the CX platform is correctly configured to act on it.

Regulatory intensity: Billing accuracy mandates, complaint resolution timelines, and outage notification requirements are intensifying. The SAP CX platform supports compliance, but only when features are correctly activated and configured.

SAP in Utilities: A Significant and Growing Market

Utilities are not a niche segment for SAP. Understanding the scale of the SAP utilities community frames why the Advanced Success Plan matters for this industry in particular. SAP is trusted by hundreds of utilities customers globally and is among the leading technology providers in the sector by installed base, a position that continues to grow year-on-year. The investment in the relationship has been made, the question is whether customers are fully using what they have.

“With the Advanced Success Plan, customers turn their SAP Customer Experience into a driver of growth and innovation. They gain the confidence to act decisively, supported by unlimited expert guidance and intelligent insights that ensure every feature delivers measurable business value.”

The Business Case: Customer Retention Over Acquisition

For utilities operating in competitive markets, retaining customers has become as strategically important as acquiring new ones. Customers can switch supplier in minutes via a digital platform; complaint escalation processes are visible on social media; and billing errors in a smart meter world are harder to excuse and faster to escalate.

The SAP CX platform addresses these challenges across the full customer lifecycle. SAP Engagement Cloud enables targeted, segmented communications. SAP Service Cloud centralizes complaint management and agent interactions. SAP Sales Cloud for managing accounts, contacts, leads, and Opportunities and SAP Commerce Cloud is the digital sales and self-service storefront layer; it handles how customers discover, compare, purchase, and manage energy products online. What the Advanced Success Plan does is ensure these capabilities are not just purchase; they are adopted, optimized, and continuously improved.

How the Advanced Success Plan Structures the Adoption Journey

The Advanced Success Plan organizes the adoption journey across four distinct phases, each with a defined purpose and a set of targeted services:

Introducing Innovation: Identify and priorities the right innovations for your business. Services here include product guidance sessions covering areas such as Utilities Session and Agent Desktop, Intelligent Selling, Campaigns and Segmentation, and AI-Foundation and Use case Navigator.

Adopting Innovation: Plan and prepare for structured adoption with minimal risk. Services include the AI Process Fit-Gap Analysis, Key Feature Advisory, Release Guidance, Success Expert engagement, Service Planning, Value Management, Innovation Checkpoints, and Adoption Checkpoints.

Activation and Optimization: Hands-on activation of AI and CX capabilities in your environment. Services include activation sessions across Sales, Service, and Marketing and Engagement clouds; AI Agent Activation; Embedded AI Activation; Joule Activation; and Technical and Functional Assistance.

Success Measurement and Continuous Improvement: Measure outcomes and sustain momentum through ongoing engagement. Services include a Continuous Engagement model, Release Guidance, Success Expert, Value Management, Innovation Checkpoints, and Adoption Checkpoints.

The Eight Services Available to Utilities Customers — and How Each One Helps

The Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience comprises eight distinct services. Each has a defined scope, a specific business need it addresses, and measurable benefits. For utilities customers, each service connects to a characteristic operational or strategic challenge.

01 — Product Guidance for SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
Structured sessions that introduce utilities teams to the capabilities most relevant to their context; from meter-read-driven billing notifications in SAP Service Cloud to segmented communications in SAP Engagement Cloud. The goal is to accelerate time-to-awareness: teams know what exists before they have to find it.

02 — Key Feature Advisory for SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
Curated, customer-specific guidance on which features to activate and in what sequence. For utilities, this means filtering a broad SAP CX roadmap down to the capabilities that matter for smart metering, complaints management, and outage communications then discarding what does not apply.

03 — Release Guidance for SAP Customer Experience Solutions
Every SAP CX quarterly release brings dozens of updates. Release Guidance ensures utilities teams receive a focused brief on what is relevant to their industry context, not a generic list of all product changes, so adoption decisions are faster and better informed.

04 — Solution Review for SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
A structured review of the current solution configuration against best practice. For utilities, this commonly surfaces configuration gaps in complaint workflows, billing notification templates, or service agent desktop layouts that erode the customer experience over time if left unaddressed.

05 — Transformation Advisory for SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
Strategic guidance that connects SAP CX capabilities to the outcome’s utilities care about most: cost-to-serve reduction, complaint resolution improvement, and regulatory compliance. Transformation Advisory moves the conversation from features to business impact.

06 — Activation for SAP Customer Experience Solutions
Hands-on, expert-assisted activation of specific capabilities in the customer's live environment. For utilities, this includes activating Joule for service agents, enabling AI-driven case categorization in Service Cloud. Many autonomous agents or assistants announced by SAP in Sapphire , once available can be activated to guide service agents to process cases.

07 — Technical Assistance for SAP Customer Experience Solutions
Direct technical support across all phases of the engagement — covering integration architecture, performance guidance, load testing guidance for mass billing cycles, and resolution of complex system behavior. This service is especially critical for utilities given the high-volume, time-sensitive nature of meter-read processing and month-end billing runs.

08 — Functional Assistance for SAP Customer Experience
Business process and functional configuration support throughout the engagement. Utilities-specific coverage includes complaint handling workflows, outage notification sequences, and the configuration of billing determinant displays in the service agent desktop, ensuring that what is built serves the way utilities actually operate.

From Idea to Impact: How the Advanced Success Plan AI Services Support Utilities Across Every Phase

Artificial intelligence is not a future aspiration for utilities — it is a present operational reality. Smart meter data, deregulated market dynamics, rising customer expectations, and regulatory complexity all create conditions where AI can deliver measurable value. But AI adoption is not a single event. It is a journey that runs from initial discovery through to continuous optimization in production. The Advanced Success Plan AI Services Catalogue provides a structured set of twelve services that guide utilities customers through exactly that journey — from understanding what is possible to running AI at scale.

 

A Practitioner's Perspective: What I See in the Field

Working directly with utilities customers, I see first-hand how the Advanced Success Plan services drive meaningful business value — especially for organizations without deep technical or functional SAP CX expertise in-house. Utility companies face a specific set of challenges: complex billing architectures, regulatory requirements, and high customer expectations for responsive service. The right services, delivered at the right moment, can be genuinely transformational. Four patterns stand out consistently.

Bridging the knowledge gap for business users: Unlike system integrators or technical consultants, many utility business teams may not fully grasp the functional nuances required to maximize their SAP investment. Value-driven CSM engagement becomes critical here: translating technical possibilities into business outcomes, advising on feature adoption, and ensuring cross-team alignment across operations, IT, and customer management. The CSM acts as connective tissue between what SAP CX can do and what the business needs to achieve.

Go-live checks: foundations for seamless launches: Utilities cannot rely on generic go-live checklists. Are meter reads flowing end-to-end? Are billing determinants and rate structures properly mapped? Are customer service orders integrated with billing and meter management systems? Comprehensive go-live checks built for the utilities context eliminate the guesswork — giving teams confidence that key processes are functioning correctly from day one, not discovered to be broken three months into the first billing cycle.

Roadmap guidance: enabling better design decisions: Each SAP CX release brings dozens of new features, but only subsets are relevant for a given customer in a given market. Regular, curated roadmap guidance helps utilities focus on the features and design decisions that will most efficiently drive their specific outcomes — whether that is improving customer satisfaction, streamlining operations, or enriching digital self-service. This targeted approach prevents costly missteps and empowers business stakeholders to make confident decisions without requiring deep product expertise of their own.

Early-phase support: building for reliability and performance: The early project phase is often when performance issues and integration risks are best addressed — but it is also where internal teams feel least certain. Proactive support that includes performance reviews spanning interconnected billing and service systems, high-level architecture guidance, and utilities-specific load testing helps ensure reliable operation under real-world peak conditions. Utilities processing millions of daily meter reads, running mass billing cycles at month-end, and handling seasonal demand spikes need to know their system will hold before the peak arrives, not after it has passed.

What Stronger SAP CX Adoption Looks Like in Practice

When utilities customers engage with the full Advanced Success Plan service portfolio at the right moments, the outcomes are concrete and measurable:

Adoption confidence: Teams understand the SAP CX capabilities that matter for their industry and know how to use them. Adoption is driven by guided enablement, not trial and error.

Configuration quality: Solution reviews and functional assistance ensure complaint workflows, billing notifications, and service processes are correctly configured — reducing workarounds and support volume.

Release relevance: Every quarterly SAP CX update is assessed for utilities relevance. Teams receive a focused brief on what to adopt — not a generic list of all product changes.

AI activation at pace: Joule, embedded AI agents, and Engagement cloud intelligence features are activated with expert assistance — moving from available-in-catalogue to live-in-production for utilities use cases.

Integration reliability: Standard integrations between SAP Service Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Utilities are correctly configured, giving agents real-time access to meter history, billing data, and service orders.

Transformation clarity: Transformation advisory connects the SAP CX roadmap to the outcomes utilities care about most: cost-to-serve reduction, complaint resolution improvement, and regulatory compliance.

A Service Portfolio Built for the Complexity of Utilities

The SAP investment in the utilities sector is substantial and well-established. Utilities organisations around the world have already deployed the SAP CX platform and are running their customer operations on it. The question is not whether they have access to world-class CX capabilities — it is whether they are fully using what they have.

The eight services described in this post, combined with CSM-led perspectives on go-live quality, roadmap relevance, and performance assurance, represent a comprehensive framework for utilities organizations to realize the full potential of their SAP CX investment. The Advanced Success Plan is not a generic offering. When applied with industry focus and CSM expertise, it becomes a strategic asset — one that helps utilities deliver on the promise of a superior customer experience in an increasingly competitive, regulated, and technically complex market.

Are you a utilities customer working through SAP CX adoption or optimization? We would welcome more conversation in the comments — whether it is a specific challenge you are navigating, a service in this portfolio you have found valuable, or a question about where to start.

entry.title: Adding an inbuilt Translation view in C4C using SAP Translation Hub service
entry.content:
Introduction

In multilingual business environments, service agents frequently receive customer emails written in languages other than their own. For example, a German‑speaking customer service representative may receive inquiries in French or Swedish. To understand the customer’s issue and provide an appropriate response, agents often rely on external translation tools. This requires translating the incoming email, taking action based on its content, drafting a reply in their own language, and then translating the response back into the customer’s preferred language.
This multi‑step process is time‑consuming, disrupts workflow, and negatively impacts overall user experience and productivity.

 

To streamline this process and enhance user efficiency, we aim to provide an integrated translation capability directly within SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C). By leveraging the SAP Translation Hub service available on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), users will be able to translate incoming and outgoing messages without navigating to external tools.
This built-in solution will significantly reduce manual effort, improve turnaround time, and deliver a seamless user experience for agents supporting multilingual customer interactions.

Architecture

Below is high level architecture that we will follow-

  • BTP app (Node.js or CAP) exposes /api/translate → calls SAP Translation Hub REST API using an OAuth token (Destination service + XSUAA).
  • Simple web UI (UI5/HTML) lets the user choose source text/language and target language; it calls /api/translate, shows the result.
  • C4C  URL Mashup embeds the web UI.

         

 

 

 

Step 1 — Provision SAP Translation Hub on BTP

a) In BTP Cockpit, entitle and create a service instance of SAP Translation Hub, Free‑tier quotas are available for trying this out.

 

 

You are now able to create service bindings for your new service instance. Service bindings are used to generate credentials to enable apps to access and communicate with the service instance.

 

In the dialog, enter a binding name, such as binding01 the name of your service binding. Click Create button to create the service binding.

 

Download this file and copy values as highlighted above. They will be used in next step while creating destination.

clientid, clientsecret, url (API endpoint), and uaa.url (token endpoint).

 

b) Review the API access model (OAuth 2.0 client credentials) and the Translate method in the API reference.

 API Reference - Software Translation | SAP Help Portal

c) On api.sap.com, you can explore SAP Translation Hub and Document Translation endpoints and their request/response shapes.

Overview | SAP Translation Hub | SAP Business Accelerator Hub 

 

Step 2 — Create a BTP Destination for Translation Hub

Create a destination (e.g., STH_REST) so your backend can call Translation Hub without hard‑coding secrets:

Go to Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination.

 

Using destinations centralizes credentials and lets the Cloud SDK or approuter acquire tokens at runtime.

 

Note:

  • Use the uaa.url value from the service key plus /oauth/token appended — e.g. https://xxxxxxx.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token.
  • Common mistake: pasting the bare uaa.url without /oauth/token, or using the url field (the API endpoint) instead of uaa.url (the auth endpoint) — these are two different keys in the service key JSON and easy to swap.
  • Set Token Service URL Type to Dedicated (not Common) unless you're deliberately sharing a token across multiple destinations.

 

Step 3 — Implement a tiny backend that calls Translation Hub

 

Below is a minimal Node.js (Express) route using the SAP Cloud SDK to call Translation Hub via the destination you created. (You can also do this in CAP; the principle is the same.)


Note: The exact payload of translate (fields like sourceLanguage, targetLanguages, textType, domain, units) is documented in the API ref; adapt the body to the Translate Method for API v2

 

// server.js
import express from 'express';
import cors from 'cors';
import { executeHttpRequest } from '@sap-cloud-sdk/http-client';

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());
app.use(cors());	

app.post('/api/translate', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { sourceLanguage, targetLanguage, text, domain, textType } = req.body;

    // Build API payload per SAP Translation Hub "translate" method (API v2)
    const payload = {
      sourceLanguage,
      targetLanguages: [targetLanguage],
      // optional depending on your use case:
      domain,        // e.g., "FIN", "CRM" – see “domains” method
      textType,      // e.g., "ui", "longtext" – see “texttypes” method
      units: [{ value: text }]
    };

    const response = await executeHttpRequest(
      { destinationName: 'STH_REST' }, // BTP Destination
      {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/v2/translate',          // adjust if your base URL already includes /v2
        data: payload,
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
      }
    );

    // Typical response has translations aligned to the requested targetLanguages
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
    res.status(500).json({ error: 'Translation failed', details: `${err}` });
  }
});

const port = process.env.PORT || 4000;
app.listen(port, () => console.log(`Translation backend running on ${port}`));

 

Step 4 — Build a simple web UI (UI5 or plain HTML)

A small form that posts to /api/translate is enough. You can deploy the static UI and backend together to Cloud Foundry behind an approuter (HTML5 repo) and expose a single HTTPS URL.

 

<!-- public/index.html -->
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8"/>
  <title>Translate (SAP Translation Hub)</title>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"/>
  <style>
    body {
      font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
      font-size: 14px;
      line-height: 1.4;
      padding: 20px;
      max-width: 800px;
      margin: 0 auto;
      color: #333;
    }
    h2 { margin-top: 0; }
    .form-group { margin-bottom: 15px; }
    label { display: inline-block; margin-right: 15px; font-weight: bold; }
    
    /* Updated styling for select and inputs */
    input, select { 
      padding: 6px; 
      border: 1px solid #ccc; 
      border-radius: 4px; 
      background-color: white;
    }
    
    textarea {
      width: 100%;
      padding: 8px;
      border: 1px solid #ccc;
      border-radius: 4px;
      box-sizing: border-box;
      resize: vertical;
    }
    button {
      background-color: #0070f2;
      color: white;
      border: none;
      padding: 10px 20px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      cursor: pointer;
      font-size: 14px;
    }
    button:hover { background-color: #005bb5; }
    button:disabled { background-color: #ccc; cursor: not-allowed; }
    #out {
      background: #f4f4f4;
      padding: 15px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      border: 1px solid #ddd;
      white-space: pre-wrap;
      margin-top: 20px;
      min-height: 50px;
    }
    .error { color: #d93025; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <h2>Translate</h2>

  <div class="form-group">
    <label>Source language:
      <select id="srcLang">
        <option value="en" selected>English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de">German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
    
    <label>Target language:
      <select id="tgtLang">
        <option value="en">English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de" selected>German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
  </div>

  <div class="form-group">
    <textarea id="srcText" rows="5" placeholder="Enter text to translate..."></textarea>
  </div>

  <button id="go" type="button">Translate</button>

  <pre id="out"></pre>

  <script>
    // Read initial values from URL params
    const qp = new URLSearchParams(location.search);
    const srcTextInput = document.getElementById('srcText');
    const tgtLangInput = document.getElementById('tgtLang');
    const srcLangInput = document.getElementById('srcLang');
    const goBtn = document.getElementById('go');
    const outPre = document.getElementById('out');

    // Initialize fields from URL if present
    if (qp.get('text')) srcTextInput.value = qp.get('text');
    
    // If URL params provide a language code that exists in our list, select it
    if (qp.get('targetLang')) tgtLangInput.value = qp.get('targetLang');
    if (qp.get('sourceLang')) srcLangInput.value = qp.get('sourceLang');

    goBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => {
      // Reset output and set loading state
      outPre.textContent = '';
      outPre.classList.remove('error');
      goBtn.disabled = true;
      const originalBtnText = goBtn.textContent;
      goBtn.textContent = 'Translating...';

      try {
        const body = {
          sourceLanguage: srcLangInput.value,
          targetLanguage: tgtLangInput.value,
          text: srcTextInput.value,
          domain: qp.get('domain') || undefined,
          textType: qp.get('textType') || 'ui'
        };

        const response = await fetch('/api/translate', {
          method: 'POST',
          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
          body: JSON.stringify(body)
        });

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error(`Server error: ${response.status} ${response.statusText}`);
        }

        const data = await response.json();
        outPre.textContent = JSON.stringify(data, null, 2);

      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Translation failed:', error);
        outPre.textContent = `Error: ${error.message}`;
        outPre.classList.add('error');
      } finally {
        // Restore button state
        goBtn.disabled = false;
        goBtn.textContent = originalBtnText;
      }
    });
  </script>
</body>
</html>

 

Step 5 — Deploy the app to BTP (Cloud Foundry)

 

  • Push the Node.js app (and static UI) to your CF space.
  • Ensure the Destination is bound/available.
  • Note the public URL (e.g., https://your-app.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/).
  • The workshop’s sample shows the same flow (basic website + microservice) jens-limbach/SSv2-extensibility-workshop: SSv2-extensibility-workshop

 

Step 6 — Create the URL Mashup in C4C  and place it on a page

 

In C4C, go to Mashup Authoring → Create → URL Mashup.

          URL: paste your BTP app URL

Place the mashup: open a record (e.g., Ticket), Start Adaptation, add a new tab/section, drop the mashup, save. Here is the preview of the new mash up. I have used very basic HTML logic to create view; further modification can be done in HTML logic to blend in the view with C4C theme.

Note: SAP C4C V2 provides native, AI‑powered language translation. If needed this can be used in C4C V2 as well.

 

Conclusion

We have explored how SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) can be enhanced with built-in translation capabilities using SAP Translation Hub on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). By implementing this solution, organizations can provide a seamless translation experience directly within C4C, enabling service agents to efficiently manage multilingual customer interactions, improve productivity, and deliver faster, more effective support.

I hope this guide helps you understand the end-to-end implementation approach and inspires further innovation in multilingual customer service scenarios. Should you have any questions, require clarification on any of the implementation steps, or wish to discuss alternative design approaches, please feel free to reach out. Your feedback and suggestions are highly valued and will help refine and strengthen this solution for broader adoption and future enhancements.

entry.title: Breaking Down Silos to Unified Customer Data — Powered by the Advanced Success Plan for SAP CX
entry.content:
More Tools, More Fragmentation

Each day, businesses invest in new software tools: marketing platforms, commerce engines, service systems, and sales technology. They are told that assembling the right combination will unlock their digital transformation journey and finally deliver that personalized and seamless experience they wish to provide to their customers.

But what if more or better software is not necessarily the answer?

Each solution solves a real problem for a specific team. But collectively, they create a consequence no one planned for: every new tool builds its own data world, without a common language or context across them. In a 2026 study by Oxford Economics, only 25% of respondents described their CX technology environment as fully harmonized and integrated. 29% remain highly fragmented. Organizations with siloed CX tech are more likely to face an inability to connect customer needs to actionable data insights. 58% reported this challenge, compared to 47% among those with harmonized environments.

More than what tools businesses choose to add to their CX landscape, how they connect and interact with each other becomes even more important. A unified data strategy sounds straightforward in principle. In practice, most businesses find that the obstacle is not ambition but rather execution. Every integration decision made without a clear data architecture becomes a future campaign mired in manual reconciliation, a customer journey that breaks at the handoff, or a personalization promise the disparate data sources cannot support. Implementation without a validated strategy creates new fragmentation inside the solution meant to eliminate the old. And without a structured way to pressure-test decisions before any commitment is made, even well-resourced organizations find themselves repeating the same cycle: invest, integrate, fragment, repeat.

A Better Way to Build

The real barrier is not budget or technology.

A Forrester study of 1,000+ senior executives found that data quality and integration issues are cited as the top cause (39%) of delayed or derailed transformation projects, more than any other factor such as budget, technology maturity, and talent. The same study found that 56% of respondents struggle with poor data quality; 55% face persistent data silos. This is not for lack of investment in technology, but because the connections between systems were not designed or maintained effectively.

What bridges that gap is not another platform — it is the expertise to think through data connectivity decisions before they are made, and the ongoing guidance to ensure those decisions compound into measurable gains over time. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience solutions provides that guidance along every step of the journey.

Define what success actually looks like

The most common reason data unification projects fall short of expectations is not technical failure – it is a failure to define and measure what success looks like for the entire business before the work begins. The Value Management session from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience establishes that definition at the outset: what does a fully unified data strategy actually enable? It means running the next marketing campaign without manual data reconciliation, and presenting an AI readiness roadmap without caveats. Stakeholders will know, at every checkpoint, whether the investment is moving the business forward, not just moving the project forward.

Design the strategy before building the integrations

A robust data strategy starts with good design. Product Guidance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience covers available out-of-the-box integrations, common usage scenarios, and pitfalls and how to avoid them — all delivered in a live remote session by an SAP expert who can answer questions in real time. The data strategy can be conceptualized and pressure-tested before any budget or technical commitments are made.

Validate every critical decision with expert guidance

With the Technical Assistance and Functional Assistance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience, businesses have continuous access to expert guidance at every critical decision point. Beyond resolving immediate questions, the ongoing access also shares insight on how SAP thinks through problems, strengthening in-house expertise with every interaction. The result is an organization that makes better decisions not just now but for the future.

Measure whether the strategy is delivering

Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints conducted on a quarterly or semi-annual basis bring the measurement back to where it started: the business outcomes defined at the outset. Do campaigns run without manual reconciliation? Is the AI use case performing against its stated goals? A clear throughline from the Value Management success KPIs to the Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints proves the benefits of the investment.

Where cycles and silos break

The cycle of invest, integrate, fragment, repeat is not inevitable. It is the predictable result of making technical decisions without an anchoring business imperative, and integration decisions without strategic expert guidance. Organizations that break the cycle do not necessarily have better technology than their competitors — they have better judgment about how to use it. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience exists for exactly that reason: to put proactive and prescriptive guidance at every decision point where that judgment matters most. When data strategy is designed before it is built, validated before it is committed, and measured against real business outcomes, the technology investment already made starts working harder. The stack was never the problem. When the thinking behind the technology finally matches its ambition, the personalized, seamless experience becomes a reality.

Where is your organization in the invest, integrate, fragment, repeat cycle? What would it require to break the cycle organizationally, not just technically?

entry.title: SAP Service Cloud Q2 '26 Release Highlights
entry.content:
SAP Service Cloud Q2 2026 Release Highlights

Explore a selection of our new features in this blog post and join our webcasts for live demos and deeper insights SAP Service Cloud Version 2 updates. Register for the Q2 release webcast here.

The momentum from SAP Sapphire 2026 continues to energize the service community. We are looking forward to bringing autonomous service to life with autonomous AI agents and assistants.  While we look ahead to these innovations, we're also continuing to deliver enhancements to your day-to-day work. This quarter brings a range of updates across SAP Service Cloud designed to simplify everyday service tasks and help service teams spend less time on process and more time on customers.

Email Editor Built For the AI-first World


Email is one of the biggest opportunities for AI to improve productivity.  A new, native email editor is now available in SAP Service Cloud—built from the ground up for an AI-first world.  Today, it gives service reps a modern, streamlined writing experience with the formatting and productivity features they expect. AI capabilities are coming next, built directly into the email experience — helping teams respond faster while keeping every response personal and on point.

Better Customer Service with Voice AI Through the Parloa Partnership

 

At Sapphire, we announced an expanded partnership with Parloa, bringing together conversational AI and SAP Service Cloud to help organizations deliver more seamless customer service experiences. By connecting AI-powered customer interactions with SAP business processes and data, companies can provide faster, more contextual support across voice and digital channels. Available today, this partnership represents an important step toward more intelligent and increasingly autonomous service operations.


Sales Objects: Broader Support in the Service Agent Workspace

 

Service interactions often reveal new customer needs and valuable buying signals. Based on strong customer demand, your service reps can now create leads, opportunities, appointments, and sales orders directly from Customer Hub—without leaving their workspace. This helps organizations bridge the gap between service and sales, deliver a more seamless customer experience and capture cross-sell and upsell opportunities.

  

Case Management: Enhanced Compliance, Usability and Quality


Case management now includes new capabilities that improve compliance, usability, and data quality.
- Organizations can now define data retention rules by case type, enabling more granular governance and compliance with business and regulatory requirements.

- Agents can copy sub-cases while preserving their relationship to the parent case, making it easier to manage complex service scenarios.

- New capabilities such as Consistency Check for Cases help service reps identify and resolve data inconsistencies, ensuring cases are complete, accurate, and ready to progress through the service process.

 

 

Integrations: Service Contracts and Service Orders


Show Service Contract and Item Information in Service Orders
Service reps can now view associated service contract and contract item information directly within service orders. This enhancement provides service teams with easier access to customer agreement details while processing service orders.

Service Contract determination
Service Cloud can now automatically determine the relevant service contract when a service order is created. This helps service teams quickly identify applicable customer agreements, entitlements, and contract coverage without manual searches, ensuring that service activities are aligned with the customer's contractual commitments.

Embedded AI: Simpler Administration And Richer Insights for Similar Cases

You can now discover and manage AI capabilities more easily with a redesigned, tile-based configuration interface. Admins can quickly access feature settings and gain a clearer overview of available AI capabilities. For service agents, Similar Case Recommendations now provide additional case details directly in the recommendation view, enabling a better understanding of recommended cases and helping agents quickly identify the most useful information.

 

Service order: improved usability and extensions

Working with service orders is now easier with enhancements that improve visibility and navigation.
Visual filters help service reps quickly focus on relevant service orders and detailed item views provide greater transparency into service order content. You can also create home page KPI cards for service orders and service quotes.


Enhanced integration capabilities help connect service orders across business applications. Administrators can create external links to related records in external systems using external ID's. We have also added support for multiple external SAP systems providing greater flexibility for organizations operating across connected SAP landscapes.


Utilities: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud utilities add-on


As announced at Sapphire, a new add-on for utilities is now available, covering sales processes in addition to the service processes already supported. This new add-on helps utility companies reduce friction between sales and service, increase efficiency and elevate customer experience. If you are interested in this topic, watch this webinar: Utilities CX Reloaded: The New Sales Process Explained

 

 

More capabilities to explore

This release also brings a range of additional improvements worth exploring. 
Administrators can now sign in as another user to troubleshoot issues faster and without friction.
Analytics capabilities have been extended, with support for embedding SAP Analytics Cloud widgets directly into daily workflows and the ability to use custom service entities as data sources.
Approval processes are now more flexible and reliable, with configurable approval and rejection reasons and automatic approval settings to keep workflows moving.

In our upcoming Q2 release webcast we will take a closer look at these and many other capabilities. Looking forward to seeing you there!

Additional resources: 
Sign up for our Sales and Service Newsletter to stay up to date with the latest information. 
Read the CX Q2 release blog

entry.title: SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2: How SAP Is Rebuilding the Future of Customer Experience
entry.content:
SAP has quietly reset the clock on its CRM platform. SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 (V2) isn't a facelift or a routine upgrade —

Transition to V2

SAP's CRM story stretches back decades — from the original WebUI, through ABAP-based CRM On-Premise, into Cloud for Customer (C4C), and eventually Sales Cloud V1. V2 is SAP's answer to all of that history: a microservices architecture, zero-downtime quarterly releases, and a clean OData V4 API layer that fully replaces the older OData V2 foundation V1 was built on.

 

A Sales Process Built Around the Rep, Not the System

Before diving into individual apps, it's worth understanding how V2 reshapes the day-to-day sales workflow itself.

The Digital Sales Workplace: A Command Center for Reps

At the center of Sales Cloud V2 sits the Digital Sales Workplace — essentially a daily command center purpose-built for sales reps. It pulls together everything a salesperson touches in a day, from prepping for calls to tracking how deals are progressing, all in one place.

The main screen combines a prioritized to-do list, an integrated calendar, and one-click access to the tools reps rely on most: call lists, tasks, opportunities, leads, emails, and the sales navigator.

That consolidation translates into speed. Reps can create accounts, contacts, tasks, appointments, leads, opportunities, and quotes — or log a call — without ever leaving the dashboard. A Kanban-style pipeline view rounds it out, letting reps drag and drop deals across stages so pipeline progress is always visible at a glance.

A Seamless Path From Lead to Quote

Consistency is what makes a sales process scalable, and V2 bakes that consistency in through a connected chain of applications that carry a deal from first contact to close. The standard flow runs through four stages:

Lead → Opportunity → Quote → Sales Order

1. Lead capture

Everything starts in the Leads app, where reps view and manage every incoming lead. Each record carries the details that matter — source (website form, event, referral), contact information, engagement history, lead score, and follow-up status — so reps can quickly judge lead quality and act: schedule a call, hand it off to a teammate, or convert it straight into an opportunity.

2. Opportunity management

Once qualified, a lead becomes an opportunity and moves into the Opportunities app, where a Kanban-style board tracks its progress across pipeline stages. From here, reps can:

  • Drag opportunities from one stage to the next (say, from "Qualification" to "Proposal")
  • Schedule follow-ups
  • Send emails without leaving the workspace
  • Book meetings or site visits
  • Log call notes and meeting summaries

3. Quote creation

When an opportunity matures, the Quotes app takes over. Here, users generate customer-specific proposals, adjust pricing and discounts, apply terms and conditions, and track the deal through its approval workflow.

4. Sales order generation

Once a quote is approved, native integration with SAP S/4HANA (or other back-end systems) converts it directly into a Sales Order — no manual re-entry required.

Turning Data Into Decisions: Analytics in V2

Good decisions start with visibility, and analytics sits at the core of how Sales Cloud V2 helps teams stay ahead of performance rather than reacting to it.

Pipeline Manager (Bubble Chart View) gives the pipeline a visual, intuitive shape: every deal appears as a bubble, sized by potential value and colored by urgency, so reps and managers can spot the opportunities that deserve attention first.

Forecast Tracker brings real-time and predictive forecasting into a single view. From here, sales managers can set and adjust forecast targets, compare actual performance against what was forecasted, and monitor trends over time.

Beyond what's built in, Predefined and Custom Reports extend the picture — standard reporting covers leads, opportunities, and sales performance, while integration with SAP Analytics Cloud opens the door to deeper, custom dashboards tailored to specific business needs.

Where AI Actually Shows Up in the Workflow

Sales Cloud V2 doesn't bolt AI on as an afterthought — it's woven through the tools reps and managers use every day.

Lead and opportunity scoring ranks each lead and opportunity using past engagement, deal history, and predictive signals, so it's immediately clear which ones are most likely to close and deserve priority in the pipeline.

Email draft recommender writes personalized email drafts in seconds. Reps can dial in the tone — formal or friendly — and the length, sending faster without starting each message from a blank page.

AI-driven recommendations step in when the next move isn't obvious. The system suggests concrete next steps — schedule a follow-up call, send a quote, offer a discount — based on what's worked in similar deals before, and it even flags upsell opportunities based on past buying behavior.

Account synopsis distills a customer account into a short, clear summary: recent activity, past purchases, open challenges, and overall sales potential, all in a single snapshot.

Predictive analytics takes reporting a step further. Tools like the Pipeline Bubble Chart and Forecast Tracker use AI to project deal outcomes, flagging high-potential and at-risk opportunities and checking real-time results against forecasts — so teams can act early rather than after the fact.

Joule, SAP's AI agent, ties it all together as a conversational assistant. Ask it something like "Which opportunities are at risk this quarter?" or "What's the forecast for Q3?" and it answers in real time, grounded in your own company's data.

The Big Q1 2026 Story: Joule Studio Goes GA

If there's one headline from this release cycle, it's this: Joule Studio reached General Availability in Q1 2026 — the single biggest development in Sales Cloud V2 since it launched. It's the shift SAP has been signaling since Sapphire 2025: moving Joule from a reactive AI copilot into a proactive agent-building platform.

What Actually Changed

Before Joule Studio, Joule behaved like a copilot — you asked, it answered. Joule Studio changes that relationship entirely by letting you build custom AI agents that run multi-step workflows on their own. These agents connect Sales Cloud V2, S/4HANA, third-party systems, and external data sources into a single automated flow, with no extra tooling and no manual handoffs in between.

💡What that looks like in practice: An agent keeps watch over all open Q2 opportunities. If a deal hasn't had customer contact in 14 days and is set to close this quarter, the agent automatically drafts a follow-up email, logs the activity, and alerts the account manager — no one had to ask it to

To go deeper on building and configuring these agents:

  • Joule Studio — Build AI Agents, Apps, and Workflows
  • What is Joule Studio? — SAP Help Portal
  • Joule Setup for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

The recommended path: start with core Joule capabilities, configure onboarding and permissions, and only then move on to designing custom agents and workflows around your own business processes.

Sales Intelligence: Scoring Deals on What Actually Happens

Q1 2026 also marked the first real leap forward in deal intelligence. Rather than relying on AI guesswork, the system now watches engagement patterns at the deal level and surfaces risk signals grounded in historical deal-slippage data — patterns pulled from real outcomes, not assumptions.

A few resources worth bookmarking on this:

Opportunity Scoring (Deal Intelligence) — SAP Help Portal. Covers overview screens showing the opportunity score, the factors influencing it, and the underlying scoring concepts. Read it here

View Deal Scoring Predictions — SAP Help Portal. The best source for screenshots of the opportunity score UI, the score popup, the insights panel, the score trend, and the risk explanation view

 

 

SAP Sales Cloud V2 — Deal Intelligence Overview. A broader product walkthrough covering opportunity scoring and pipeline acceleration. Read it here

Guided selling ties this all together, giving reps account insights and timelines, relationship intelligence, and prescriptive next-best-action recommendations — while reinforcing good selling habits through embedded gamification

Pipeline Manager Visualization shows exactly how Deal Intelligence surfaces inside pipeline charts and opportunity views, blending the bubble chart with score visualization.

Analytics, Rebuilt From the Ground Up

Q1 2026's analytics update went beyond dashboards. Redesigned forecasting views now support multi-level drill-down, and V2's analytics layer introduces intent-based navigation — the ability to jump from an embedded SAC story straight to the exact CRM record you need to review or update, in just two clicks.

The December 2025 release laid the groundwork for this: it added currency conversion for amount-based attributes inside embedded SAC stories, along with a live connection to Enterprise SAC that keeps data in the source system and discards it once the story closes — a meaningful step forward for data security.

Release History: What's Landed So Far

Q1 2026 — Joule Studio GA + Deal Intelligence + Forecasting Rebuild

The custom AI agent builder goes generally available, joined by activity-based risk scoring, multi-level forecast drill-down, and improvements to mobile and CTI.

December 2025 — Enterprise SAC Live Connection + Currency Conversion

A real-time live connection to Enterprise SAP Analytics Cloud, plus currency conversion support for global reporting inside embedded SAC stories.

2508 — WalkMe Integration + Migration & Adoption Improvements

This release focused squarely on adoption, onboarding, and transition support across both Sales and Service Cloud. Highlights included:

  • WalkMe integration for guided in-app user journeys
  • Smart content flows for feature discovery and onboarding
  • An improved Readiness Check and better Data Transfer tooling
  • Stronger support for V1 → V2 transition planning

2508 — Opportunity Management & Guided Selling Enhancements

Sales execution kept evolving with sharper opportunity management and guidance experiences:

  • Enhancements to Opportunity Management
  • Improved Guided Selling scenarios
  • Better opportunity execution support
  • A more productive seller workspace experience

2508 — Service Operations & Integration Improvements

Service teams saw updates aimed at operational efficiency and more connected experiences:

  • Ticket management enhancements
  • Integration improvements across service processes
  • Faster navigation and execution
  • Improved visibility into service operations

Q1 2026 — Joule Studio GA + Deal Intelligence + Forecasting Rebuild

Custom AI agent capabilities went generally available alongside deeper built-in intelligence:

  • Build and manage custom AI agents with Joule Studio
  • Activity-based opportunity risk scoring
  • Forecast drill-down with improved analytics
  • Mobile and CTI enhancements

December 2025 — Enterprise SAC Live Connection

Analytics became more scalable across global organizations:

  • Live connection to Enterprise SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Reduced duplication of analytical data

More secure reporting experiences

Q3 2025 — Business Information Extraction Extended to Cases

AI capabilities expanded into service execution:

  • Automatic extraction of business entities from customer interactions
  • Smarter case categorization
  • ML-generated interaction summaries
  • Resolution insights paired with sentiment awareness

September 2025 — Intent-Based Navigation in Analytics

Analytics moved from pure reporting toward action-oriented workflows:

  • Direct navigation from analytics to CRM business objects
  • Faster movement from insight to execution
  • Reduced context switching
  • Improved operational productivity

Where This Is All Heading

Across SAP Customer Experience, the innovation roadmap keeps circling back to three themes:

  • Sales → Intelligent Revenue Execution
  • Service → AI-Assisted Resolution
  • ESM → Connected Enterprise Service Delivery

For more release detail and screenshots, see the SAP Sales and Service Cloud 2508 Release Briefing.

The takeaway: SAP Sales Cloud V2 isn't just an incremental release cycle — it's a platform that's actively becoming more autonomous, more predictive, and more integrated with every quarter. For teams starting fresh, V2 is the clear starting point.
